title: Retrieval with Feedback Loop
one_liner: Collects user relevance/quality ratings on RAG answers, uses an LLM to match past feedback to new queries for score-based re-ranking, and periodically folds highly-rated Q&A pairs back into the vector index.
problem_solved: A static RAG system never learns: it keeps retrieving the same marginal chunks and repeating the same bad answers no matter how often users signal dissatisfaction. This technique closes the loop by persisting per-answer user feedback (relevance and quality scores 1-5), using that history to re-weight and re-rank retrieved documents on future queries, and periodically enriching the vector store with query+answer text from highly-rated interactions so proven-good content becomes directly retrievable.
failure_modes: My RAG system keeps giving the same bad or irrelevant answers to recurring questions even after users flag them
Retrieval quality never improves over time because the system has no memory of which answers users found useful
Documents that users consistently rate as unhelpful keep ranking at the top of retrieval results
Good answers the system already produced are 'lost' - the same question later retrieves worse context and regenerates a worse answer
The system cannot adapt to what my specific user group actually considers relevant versus what embedding similarity says
when_to_use: You run a long-lived, multi-user RAG application (support bot, internal knowledge assistant) where the same or similar questions recur and you can collect thumbs-up/down or 1-5 ratings
Users' notion of a 'good answer' diverges from raw embedding similarity and you want the ranking to drift toward observed user preference
Your knowledge base is fairly static but answer quality complaints persist - the gain must come from ranking/curation rather than new documents
You want a lightweight online-learning effect without retraining embedding models or building a learning-to-rank pipeline
You already log query/response pairs and adding a feedback field is cheap
tradeoffs: Expensive at query time: the relevance check calls the LLM once per (retrieved doc x stored feedback entry), so cost and latency grow linearly with feedback history - O(docs*feedback) GPT-4o calls per query becomes untenable without pruning, caching, or a cheaper classifier
Needs real user feedback volume; useless for one-off scripts, cold-start systems, or low-traffic apps where feedback is sparse
Feedback quality risk: noisy, biased, or adversarial ratings directly reshape ranking and get baked into the index during fine-tuning

Index 'fine-tuning' here is naive - it concatenates highly-rated query+answer strings into the corpus and rebuilds the whole vector store, which can inject model-generated (possibly hallucinated) text into the retrieval corpus and requires periodic full re-indexing

The notebook's score adjustment assumes docs carry a 'relevance_score' in metadata and mutates retriever.search_kwargs in a way that is illustrative rather than production-ready; real deployment needs a custom retriever/re-ranker layer
Adds state management (feedback JSON store, re-index schedule) and thus operational complexity compared to stateless RAG
how_it_works: Setup: a PDF is read to a string (read_pdf_to_string from repo helper_functions), embedded into a FAISS-style vector store (encode_from_string), and wrapped in a LangChain RetrievalQA chain with ChatOpenAI gpt-4o. Loop step 1 - collect: after each answer, get_user_feedback() packages {query, response, relevance 1-5, quality 1-5, comments} and store_feedback() appends it as a JSON line to data/feedback_data.json. Loop step 2 - re-rank (post-retrieval): on a new query, adjust_relevance_scores() iterates over every retrieved doc and every stored feedback entry, asking gpt-4o (via a PromptTemplate piped to llm.with_structured_output(Response) with a Yes/No Pydantic schema) whether that past feedback is relevant to the current query+doc; for docs with relevant feedback it multiplies doc.metadata['relevance_score'] by avg_relevance/3 (3 = neutral on the 1-5 scale) and re-sorts docs by adjusted score, then stuffs the adjusted docs back into retriever.search_kwargs. Loop step 3 - periodic index update (indexing stage): fine_tune_index() filters feedback with relevance>=4 and quality>=4, concatenates their query+response text onto the original corpus, and rebuilds the vector store with encode_from_string, swapping in a new retriever. The technique therefore spans post-retrieval (dominant: feedback-driven re-ranking) plus indexing (periodic corpus enrichment) and orchestration (the ongoing collect-adjust-refresh cycle).
key_dependencies: OpenAI API (gpt-4o for generation and feedback-relevance judging)
repo helper_functions.py (read_pdf_to_string, encode_from_string - FAISS + OpenAI embeddings under the hood)
json / local JSONL file as the feedback store
key_code_signals: store_feedback(feedback) appending {query, response, relevance, quality, comments} as JSON lines to data/feedback_data.json
adjust_relevance_scores(query, docs, feedback_data) - nested loop calling the LLM per (doc, feedback) pair
relevance_chain = relevance_prompt | llm.with_structured_output(Response) with a Yes/No Pydantic Response model
doc.metadata['relevance_score'] *= (avg_relevance / 3) followed by sorted(docs, key=relevance_score, reverse=True)
fine_tune_index(feedback_data, texts) filtering relevance >= 4 and quality >= 4 and rebuilding via encode_from_string(all_texts)
retriever.search_kwargs['docs'] = adjusted_docs to push re-ranked docs back into the retriever
related_techniques: reranking: refines - both re-order retrieved docs post-retrieval, but this uses accumulated user feedback instead of a cross-encoder/LLM relevance model per query
adaptive_retrieval: sibling - same 'Iterative Techniques' repo category; adapts retrieval per query type at query time rather than learning across sessions from feedback
retrieval_with_feedback_loop vs self_rag: alternative - Self-RAG uses the model's own reflection tokens as the feedback signal within one query, this uses human ratings across queries
simple_rag: prerequisite - the base vectorstore + RetrievalQA pipeline this technique wraps
relevant_segment_extraction / contextual_compression: composes-with - feedback re-ranking can precede context filtering before generation
choose_chunk_size / simple_rag indexing: composes-with - fine_tune_index rebuilds the same encode_from_string index with feedback-derived documents
summary: Retrieval with Feedback Loop makes a RAG system learn from its users instead of staying static. Every answer is rated (relevance and quality, 1-5) and appended to a JSONL feedback store. On later queries, an LLM judge (gpt-4o with structured Yes/No output) checks each stored feedback entry against each retrieved document and the current query; documents backed by positively-rated past interactions get their relevance_score metadata multiplied up (avg_relevance/3), negatively-rated ones get demoted, and the doc list is re-sorted before generation. Periodically, feedback with relevance>=4 and quality>=4 is converted into new corpus text (query + answer concatenated) and the vector store is rebuilt, so proven-good answers become directly retrievable. It addresses the failure where a RAG system repeats bad answers and never adapts to user preference. Costs: O(docs x feedback entries) LLM calls per query, dependence on sufficient honest feedback volume, risk of injecting model-generated text into the index, and full periodic re-indexing. Best for long-lived, recurring-question applications; wrong for cold-start or low-traffic systems.
notes_for_chunking: Well-structured for chunking: cell 1 is a single large, self-contained markdown overview (Overview / Motivation / Key Components / Method Details / Benefits / Conclusion with ### subsections) that alone could serve as the concept chunk. After that, the notebook alternates one short '### ...' markdown header cell with one code cell per function (get_user_feedback, store_feedback, load_feedback_data, adjust_relevance_scores, fine_tune_index, demo, periodic fine-tune) - ideal header+code pairing; keep each header cell glued to its following code cell. Markdown/code ratio roughly 50/50 by cell count but code dominates by tokens in the second half. Junk to strip: cell 0 (book advertisement), cell 2 (SVG image embed), cell 4 (pip install), cell 5 (git clone + sys.path Colab boilerplate), cell 8 (wget data downloads), and the final tracking-pixel markdown cell. No cell outputs present, so no giant output stripping needed. The wildcard imports 'from helper_functions import *' hide key functions (read_pdf_to_string, encode_from_string, PromptTemplate, BaseModel come from there implicitly) - note this when attributing dependencies. adjust_relevance_scores and fine_tune_index are the two load-bearing code cells; each is self-contained enough to embed as its own chunk with its header.
